title: Permission denied when using --user and mounting /etc/passwd:ro
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

After upgrading to podman 3.3.0 on Fedora 34 I now observe when starting a postgresql container with my own user id with the `--user` option and mounted `/etc/passwd:ro` that the container can't access /var/lib/postgresql/data inside the container anymore. I guess this has a very good security reason and I guess it somewhat specific to containers like postgresql and their user / process behavior (how they control which user starts the process inside the container etc.). Nevertheless I'd like to know what changed exactly 


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

```
$ podman run --rm -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD="test" -e POSTGRES_USER="$(id -u -n)" -e POSTGRES_DB="test" --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" -v /etc/passwd:/etc/passwd:ro docker.io/library/postgres:13
```

**Describe the results you received:**

Since the update to 3.3.0 the init script of the postgresql container complains about permission denied errors when trying to update file ownership of the database.

```
$ podman run --rm -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD="test" -e POSTGRES_USER="$(id -u -n)" -e POSTGRES_DB="test" --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" -v /etc/passwd:/etc/passwd:ro docker.io/library/postgres:13
chmod: changing permissions of '/var/lib/postgresql/data': Operation not permitted
chmod: changing permissions of '/var/run/postgresql': Operation not permitted
The files belonging to this database system will be owned by user "postgres".
This user must also own the server process.

The database cluster will be initialized with locale "en_US.utf8".
The default database encoding has accordingly been set to "UTF8".
The default text search configuration will be set to "english".

Data page checksums are disabled.

fixing permissions on existing directory /var/lib/postgresql/data ... initdb: error: could not change permissions of directory "/var/lib/postgresql/data": Operation not permitted
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

```
$ podman run --rm -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD="test" -e POSTGRES_USER="$(id -u -n)" -e POSTGRES_DB="test" --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" -v /etc/passwd:/etc/passwd:ro docker.io/library/postgres:13
...
PostgreSQL init process complete; ready for start up.

2021-09-03 08:55:55.870 UTC [1] LOG:  starting PostgreSQL 13.4 (Debian 13.4-1.pgdg100+1) on x86_64-pc-linux-gnu, compiled by gcc (Debian 8.3.0-6) 8.3.0, 64-bit
2021-09-03 08:55:55.870 UTC [1] LOG:  listening on IPv4 address "0.0.0.0", port 5432
2021-09-03 08:55:55.870 UTC [1] LOG:  listening on IPv6 address "::", port 5432
2021-09-03 08:55:55.874 UTC [1] LOG:  listening on Unix socket "/var/run/postgresql/.s.PGSQL.5432"
2021-09-03 08:55:55.884 UTC [70] LOG:  database system was shut down at 2021-09-03 08:55:55 UTC
2021-09-03 08:55:55.903 UTC [1] LOG:  database system is ready to accept connections
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.0
API Version:  3.3.0
Go Version:   go1.16.6
Built:        Fri Aug 20 21:36:14 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**

No

comments.body: Can you try running with `--privileged` and check if that works.
`--privileged` does not work as `--security-opt label=disable`. Also using `sudo ...` does not work in either of these cases.
And it works when you remove `-v /etc/passwd:/etc/passwd:ro`?

No it does not. Seems to be tied to the `--user "$(id -u):$(id -g)"` option.

Out of curiosity I also tried a bind mount on a new folder with `container_file_t` mounted to `/var/lib/postgresql/data` but the result is the same. I thought that at least that should work.

Omitting the `--user` works.

When providing the `/etc/passwd:ro` mount but not the `--user` option I get a complaint that `‘postgres’ is not the name of a known user` which makes sense because I don't have a postgres user on my host

Interesting: running the command without user options or passwd mount but with a volume to `/var/lib/postgresql/data` works, the bind mount directory's user is changed to I guess what is a random id of the podman mapper magic `100998` and it's permissions are changed to `0700` which also means although I created the container as user, rootless without any --privileged flag or CAPs I'm no longer able to interact, open or remove the directory used in the bind mount

The user obviously cannot chmod the directory if it is not the owner so I do not understand how this could have worked with previous versions.

This might help to understand the rootless volumes https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/debug-rootless-podman-mounted-volumes

Thanks for the read. 

Since before 3.3.0 the process executed in the postgresql container under my user id was able to chmod the directory `/var/lib/postgresql/data` to my user id inside the container (without the bind mount) there has to be an explanation. I'm not firm enough about the whole access control and id mapper mechanics in podman to understand what changed though
Yes something must have changed, are you it is the podman version and not the image?

Yes I'm very certain. The problem occurred right after the update to podman v3.3.0 without pulling a new version of the container in between. I double checked by downloading an older version of the container. I also downgraded from podman 3.3.0 to 3.1.0 and verified it works with that version.

I suppose if this is something that should not have worked in the first place and I "misused" podman here that probably I "exploited" some bug that got fixed in 3.3.0.
There was a change to set the ownership of buildin volumes to match the existing ownership in the image.

I am trying to see where the chown commands are happening, this is strange.
/usr/local/bin/docker-entrypoint.sh
Which has
```
	# allow the container to be started with `--user`
	if [ "$user" = '0' ]; then
		find "$PGDATA" \! -user postgres -exec chown postgres '{}' +
		find /var/run/postgresql \! -user postgres -exec chown postgres 
'{}' +
	fi
```
If you run without the --user option, it works in rootless mode.
Also it seems the /etc/passwd mount is not necessary and is actually no readable within the container, if you are using SELinux.
@rhatdan I see. Thank you for clearing that up! So mounting that passwd file was wrong from the start and using the --user flag worked as expected because of a bug that is now fixed.
